frameworks.name: Pick Your Price
frameworks.definition: A three-stage offer structure: (1) market the offer as free, (2) at checkout present three donation/payment tiers (small/medium/large) with escalating bonuses, (3) end with a payment method question and silence. The framing attributes bonuses to staff, not the business, to exploit the consumer preference for paying employees over businesses.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi reports that during COVID, gyms using the Pick Your Price scripting in their sales process achieved an average ticket of approximately $99, which exceeded the average ticket for standard low-barrier offers such as a boot camp trial.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: sales staff who could not normally close

numbers.axis: average ticket on low-barrier gym offer

anchors.region: standard boot camp or similar low-barrier offer (paid)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when consumers are told their payment goes to employees (or staff families) rather than the business, they feel significantly better about paying and are more willing to pay the highest tier, even though businesses pay their employees regardless.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: bonuses attributed to staff, not business
claims.mechanism: Consumers have a psychological preference for paying individuals over businesses; framing payment as benefiting staff bypasses this resistance and increases willingness to pay the premium tier.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the Pick Your Price offer structure has a very high conversion rate relative to other attraction offers, though the average ticket is typically lower, making it particularly effective in low-trust environments where the seller lacks brand authority.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Goodwill loaded into the free offer reduces buyer resistance; the choice framing makes prospects feel in control, which increases willingness to engage.
bounded_by: Average ticket is typically lower than with other attraction offers
Did not qualify for inclusion in $100M Offers as a universal-fit model

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the Pick Your Price offer sets the stage for future upsells because it initiates a relationship based on goodwill and reciprocity rather than a pure transactional exchange.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Goodwill established during the free/donation-framed offer creates a relational foundation that reduces friction for subsequent upsells; a money model is a series of offers, not a single transaction.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in Pick Your Price offers, tiered bonuses should be structured so that the free tier has low operational cost to the business, while higher-cost deliverables are reserved for paying tiers, preventing staff burnout.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that stating a price range (e.g., $149-$299) instead of a fixed price causes most buyers to pay above the midpoint because they do not want to appear cheap or unsupportive, effectively creating a social-proof-driven price floor.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: art gallery or similar context with discretionary pricing
payment framed as supporting the creator or artist
claims.mechanism: Buyers anchor to the midpoint of the range as the social norm; paying below it signals cheapness, so most overshoot the midpoint to signal generosity or support.
bounded_by: More niche application; not universally applicable to all business models

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the Pick Your Price offer should be pre-framed at the start of the interaction by informing prospects that a sliding-scale donation structure exists and that staff are offering extra bonuses at each tier, so there is no awkwardness when payment is requested at the end.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Upfront disclosure of the donation framing avoids surprise at checkout and builds goodwill by being transparent; it also gives the business credit for generosity before any payment is discussed.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the standard close at the end of a Pick Your Price offer should be: name the accepted payment methods and then stay silent; the prospect will announce which tier they want because it is psychologically difficult to say no to a free offer that has already been framed as a choice.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller has already delivered the free tier framing
claims.mechanism: The free base offer removes the primary objection (cost); naming payment methods and going silent forces the prospect to declare a tier choice rather than reject, because rejection is psychologically harder when the offer was positioned as a gift.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that for ongoing service relationships (not transactional ones like a car wash), the Pick Your Price process should include confrontational qualifying questions to screen for long-term commitment, because the ideal client pays willingly and thinks reciprocally.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Qualifying questions during the free-offer stage weed out low-commitment prospects before operational cost is incurred; reciprocally-minded clients generate better long-term LTV.
bounded_by: Qualifying step is not applicable to one-time transactional contexts like a car wash

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that the Pick Your Price mechanism pulls on generosity and reciprocity rather than imposing a demand, making buyers feel 100% in control of the outcome, which he claims reduces sales resistance compared to standard priced offers.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Choice architecture (pick your own price) activates autonomy rather than compliance; buyers who feel in control are less likely to resist the transaction and more likely to pay the highest tier to signal their values.

examples.text: During COVID in Austin, Texas, Alex Hormozi and his wife Layla drove past a girl holding a 'Free Car Wash' sign. At the car wash, a man explained that the standard wash was free but that they were accepting donations for the staff. He showed a pricing board with tiers (wax at $30+, hand buff at $67+, full interior at $99+) and went silent after his pitch. Hormozi paid $20 and noted the total exceeded what the most expensive automated wash would normally charge. He described immediately seeing the business model as brilliant: high goodwill, high cashflow, high margin, on a normally commoditized service.

A lemonade stand example: the base offer is a free lemonade cup. The upsell is a donation to help employees' families. Bonus tiers escalate: hand-squeezed lemonade for any donation over $5, a photo print for donations above $25, and three months of lemonade shipments for $99+. This illustrates the three-tier escalating-bonus structure of Pick Your Price applied to the simplest possible service.

A weight-loss coaching example: the free offer is a 21-day weight loss program. The framing states most people pay $99. Tiers escalate: at $99, a one-on-one call is added; at $199, a supplement handbook is included; at $499, a guarantee of 10-pound loss is added, with a credit applied to any service if the guarantee is not met. This example shows how the Pick Your Price structure layers conditional risk-reduction (guarantees) into higher tiers.

A dentist example: the free offer is a dental cleaning. The framing uses the phrase 'most people pay $99,' which serves as a social-proof anchor communicating the norm without mandating it. Tiers add escalating services at $99 and $299. This example highlights the specific phrase 'most people pay' as the key social-norming mechanism in the Pick Your Price close.

An art gallery used a paid variant of Pick Your Price by displaying a price range (e.g., $149-$299) on every piece and inviting buyers to choose how much to support the artist. The gallery owner told Hormozi that most buyers paid above the midpoint because they did not want to appear cheap or unsupportive. Hormozi described this as 'half goodwill, half capitalism' and a permutation of the Pick Your Price structure.

practices.text: When implementing Pick Your Price, structure three bonus tiers (small, medium, large) to incentivize paying something above zero; always deliver the free tier to anyone who chooses not to pay, then upsell them on other products and services during their time with you.
Ensure the free tier deliverable has low operational cost so staff can fulfill it at scale without burnout; reserve higher-operational-cost bonuses for the paying tiers.
At the end of the Pick Your Price pitch, name the accepted payment methods (Visa, MasterCard, or equivalent) and then go silent; let the prospect declare their chosen tier.

For relational (not transactional) Pick Your Price offers, add confrontational qualifying questions during the pitch to screen for long-term commitment: ask whether the prospect will attend appointments, change behavior, or stay consistent when life gets busy.
